title: Best Portable Power Banks of 2026: Fast Charging Picks
excerpt: Portable power banks in 2026 deliver up to 140W output and 26,800mAh capacity. We tested 15+ models to find the best for phones, laptops, and daily travel.
author: CompareElite Team
intro:
Picking the wrong portable power bank means watching your phone drop to 0% two hours into a flight, waiting 4 hours for a slow trickle charge, or carrying a brick that weighs more than a book. The best portable power banks of 2026 have made serious leaps in fast charging output and capacity density — but a $22 model and an $89 model are not interchangeable, and choosing based on mAh alone will leave you frustrated. With dozens of options flooding Amazon, knowing which specs actually translate to real-world performance is the difference between staying connected and being stranded.

We tested more than 15 power banks across six criteria: total usable capacity versus rated capacity, maximum output wattage under sustained load, port configuration and cable compatibility, physical size and weight for daily carry, passthrough charging support, and safety certifications including UL and CE. Our lineup covers the Anker 737 Power Bank at the flagship end, the ultra-portable Anker Nano Power Bank 10K, the multi-device Baseus 65W Power Bank 20K, and the budget-friendly INIU Power Bank 22.5W — covering laptop users, light travelers, and cost-conscious buyers alike.

This guide includes a ranked comparison table for fast spec lookups, full individual reviews for each model, a buying guide covering the six factors that determine real-world performance, and a FAQ. Every power bank listed here is available on Amazon with current pricing and Prime-eligible fast shipping.

buying_guide.title: Total Capacity and Charging Cycles
buying_guide.body: Rated mAh capacity is measured at 3.7V internally, but your device charges at 5V — meaning actual usable capacity is typically 60-70% of the advertised number after voltage conversion losses. A 26,800mAh bank like the Anker 737 delivers roughly 16,000-18,000mAh of usable output — enough for 3-4 full iPhone charges. Most quality cells are rated for 500 charging cycles before capacity drops below 80%. The Anker 737 and Baseus 65W use high-grade lithium-ion cells tested to industry standards. Avoid no-name brands that overstate capacity with no third-party verification.

buying_guide.title: Fast Charging Output Wattage
buying_guide.body: Output wattage determines how quickly your device charges. Standard USB-A delivers 5W — too slow for most use cases. USB-C Power Delivery starts at 18W for phones and goes up to 140W for laptops. The Anker 737 Power Bank tops this guide at 140W USB-C output, enough to charge a MacBook Pro at near-wall speed. The Baseus 65W delivers 65W PD — sufficient for most ultrabooks and all smartphones. The Anker Nano Power Bank 10K outputs 30W, ideal for fast-charging phones. The INIU Power Bank delivers 22.5W — fast enough for phones but insufficient for laptops.

buying_guide.title: Number of Ports and Cable Types
buying_guide.body: Multiple ports let you charge several devices simultaneously, but total output wattage is shared across all active ports. Look for at least one high-wattage USB-C PD port plus a USB-A port for legacy cables. The Baseus 65W Power Bank 20K leads this guide with 4 ports — two USB-C and two USB-A — making it the best option for charging a laptop, phone, and earbuds simultaneously. The Anker 737 features two USB-C PD ports and one USB-A. The Anker Nano Power Bank 10K has just one port but includes a built-in USB-C cable, eliminating cable dependency for phone users.

buying_guide.title: Size and Weight for Travel
buying_guide.body: Power bank size is a direct tradeoff against capacity. Jacket-pocket portability requires under 200g and a slim form factor. The Anker Nano Power Bank 10K is the smallest in this guide at roughly credit-card thickness and under 180g — it disappears into any bag. The INIU Power Bank 22.5W is similarly slim at 10,000mAh. The Baseus 65W at 20,000mAh weighs around 450g — manageable in a backpack but too heavy for a pocket. The Anker 737 at 26,800mAh weighs 680g (1.5 lbs) and requires dedicated bag space. Match capacity to your actual carry habits, not your worst-case scenario.

buying_guide.title: Passthrough Charging Support
buying_guide.body: Passthrough charging lets you plug the power bank into a wall outlet while it simultaneously charges your connected devices. This is essential for desk setups and travel where outlet access is limited. The Anker 737 Power Bank supports passthrough charging at full output speed — plug into the wall and keep charging your laptop and phone without swapping cables. The Baseus 65W Power Bank 20K also supports passthrough. The Anker Nano Power Bank 10K and INIU Power Bank 22.5W have limited or no passthrough support. If you plan to use your power bank as a desktop charging hub, verify passthrough before buying.

buying_guide.title: Safety Certifications
buying_guide.body: Lithium-ion batteries carry genuine fire and explosion risks if built to poor standards. Look for UL certification (US safety standard), CE marking (EU), and built-in protections: overcharge protection, short-circuit protection, temperature control, and over-voltage cutoff. The Anker 737 carries UL certification and Anker's proprietary PowerIQ 4.0 safety technology, which monitors charging in real time. The Baseus 65W and INIU Power Bank both carry CE and RoHS certifications. Avoid power banks from unknown brands lacking any third-party certification — the cost savings are not worth the risk of a defective cell.

verdict: The Anker 737 Power Bank at $89 is the top pick in this guide for two reasons that no competitor at this size matches: 140W USB-C output that charges MacBook-class laptops at near-wall speed, and a 26,800mAh capacity with a smart display showing precise battery percentage. For budget buyers who need a daily phone companion, the INIU Power Bank 22.5W at $22 delivers genuine fast charging and a slim design for less than a quarter of the flagship price. Both picks and the full lineup are available on Amazon with Prime shipping — current prices are listed in this guide and reflect real-time Amazon availability.

products.name: Anker 737 Power Bank (PowerCore 24K)
pros: At 140W USB-C output, the Anker 737 charges a MacBook Pro at near-wall speed — the highest output of any power bank in this guide and the only one capable of laptop charging at full performance.
The 26,800mAh capacity delivers approximately 16,000–18,000mAh of usable output after voltage conversion losses — enough for 3–4 full iPhone charges or 1 full MacBook Air top-up per cycle.
A smart digital display shows remaining battery percentage precisely rather than dot indicators, enabling accurate planning of how many device charges remain before the next recharge.
cons: At 680g (1.5 lbs), the Anker 737 requires dedicated bag space and is not suitable for jacket pocket or single-pocket carry during day use.
At $89, the Anker 737 costs more than four times the INIU Power Bank — a premium only justified for users who need 140W laptop charging or multi-day high-capacity travel.

products.name: Anker Nano Power Bank 10K
pros: The Anker Nano Power Bank 10K measures approximately credit-card thickness and weighs under 180g — the slimmest and lightest unit in this guide, fitting into a shirt pocket without noticeable bulk.
A built-in USB-C cable eliminates cable dependency for phone charging, reducing the number of accessories needed in a carry bag to zero for users with USB-C devices.
At 30W USB-C output, the Anker Nano Power Bank 10K fast-charges most modern smartphones from 0% to 50% in approximately 30 minutes.
cons: The 10,000mAh capacity delivers approximately 6,000–7,000mAh of usable output — sufficient for about 1.5 iPhone charges, limiting it to single-day use for heavy phone users.
The single USB-C port prevents simultaneous multi-device charging and does not support passthrough charging, restricting its use as a desk hub.

products.name: Baseus 65W Power Bank 20K
pros: With 4 ports — two USB-C PD and two USB-A — the Baseus 65W supports simultaneous charging of a laptop, phone, earbuds, and smartwatch from a single 20,000mAh bank.
At 65W USB-C output, the Baseus Power Bank 20K charges most ultrabooks and all smartphones at or near wall speed — the highest laptop-compatible wattage available under $60.
The 20,000mAh capacity delivers approximately 12,000–14,000mAh usable — roughly 3 full iPhone charges or 1 complete laptop top-up — in a form factor manageable in any daypack.
cons: The Baseus 65W Power Bank generates noticeable warmth during simultaneous multi-device charging at full speed, which is expected for high-wattage lithium-ion operation but warrants attention in hot ambient conditions.

products.name: INIU Power Bank 22.5W 10K
pros: At 22.5W USB-C output, the INIU Power Bank fast-charges compatible smartphones from 0% to 50% in approximately 25–30 minutes — significantly faster than the 5W USB-A charging it replaces.
A built-in LED display shows remaining capacity as a percentage, enabling precise planning of available charge without relying on dot indicators.
At $22 with 10,000mAh capacity, 22.5W fast charging, and a slim design under 180g, the INIU Power Bank is the most affordable fast-charging option in this guide.
cons: The INIU Power Bank 22.5W does not support laptop charging — its 22.5W output falls below the 45W USB-C PD minimum required to power most ultrabooks.
Self-recharge takes 4 or more hours due to the limited USB-C input rate, making overnight charging necessary after a full day of heavy use.

products.name: Anker Prime Power Bank (200W, 20000mAh)
pros: The Anker Prime delivers 200W total output across 2 USB-C and 1 USB-A ports simultaneously — enough to fast-charge a 16-inch MacBook Pro (140W) and an iPhone 16 (27W) at full speed at the same time, a capability no other power bank under $100 in this guide matches.
A 200W USB-C port charges the 20,000mAh internal battery in approximately 75 minutes with a compatible 100W+ charger — 4× faster than the 5-hour recharge time of the Baseus 65W at 65W input.

The smart digital display shows real-time input wattage, output wattage, remaining capacity in percentage, and estimated time-to-empty simultaneously — providing visibility that the Anker 737's single-line display cannot match.
cons: At 1.51 lbs, the Anker Prime weighs 0.3 lbs more than the Baseus 65W (1.21 lbs) — a meaningful difference when carried daily in a backpack alongside a laptop and other accessories.

The 200W output requires a USB-C PD 3.0 or 3.1 source charger rated above 100W to charge at full speed — charging from a standard 65W brick reduces input to 65W and extends full recharge time to approximately 185 minutes.

products.name: UGREEN Nexode 145W Power Bank (25000mAh)
pros: The UGREEN Nexode 145W provides 25,000mAh capacity — 5,000mAh more than the Anker Prime and Baseus 65W at an identical or lower price — enabling approximately 1.3 full charges of a 15-inch MacBook Pro (100Wh) alongside 2.5 full iPhone 16 Pro charges.

A single USB-C1 port delivers up to 140W output directly to laptops requiring PD 3.1 power — charging a 16-inch MacBook Pro from 0% to 56% in 30 minutes, matching wall-charger speed in a portable unit under $80.

The digital display shows battery percentage, input wattage, and output wattage on a single screen without cycling through modes, enabling instant status checks in under 2 seconds — faster than the Anker Nano's LED indicator system that requires counting light pulses.
cons: The UGREEN Nexode 145W weighs 1.69 lbs — 0.49 lbs heavier than the Anker Nano Power Bank 10K at 0.47 lbs — making it primarily suitable for carry-on luggage and laptop bags rather than light daypacks or pants pockets.

Maximum input for recharging is 65W via USB-C, meaning the 25,000mAh battery takes approximately 240 minutes to fully recharge — roughly 1 hour and 45 minutes longer than the Anker Prime's 75-minute recharge at 200W input.

faq.q: Which portable power bank is the best overall in 2026?
faq.a: The Anker 737 Power Bank at $89 is the best portable power bank of 2026. Its 140W USB-C output is the highest tested and the only one capable of charging a MacBook Pro at near-wall speed. The 26,800mAh capacity delivers approximately 16,000–18,000mAh usable — enough for 3–4 full iPhone charges or a complete laptop top-up. The smart digital display shows precise battery percentage. For buyers who prioritize portability over capacity, the Anker Nano Power Bank 10K at $35 is the better choice at under 180g.

faq.q: What should I look for when buying a portable power bank?
faq.a: The three most important factors are usable capacity, output wattage, and port configuration. Usable capacity is 60–70% of the rated mAh due to voltage conversion losses — a 10,000mAh bank delivers roughly 6,000–7,000mAh usable. Output wattage determines charging speed: 22.5W is fast for phones, 65W charges most laptops, 140W handles all USB-C laptops. Port count matters if you charge multiple devices — the Baseus 65W at $59 offers 4 ports. Also verify safety certifications: UL certification and built-in overcharge protection are non-negotiable for safe daily use.

faq.q: Is it worth spending $60 or more on a portable power bank?
faq.a: Spending $60 or more is justified if you need laptop charging, multi-port capability, or high capacity for multi-day travel. The Baseus 65W at $59 adds laptop charging at 65W and 4 ports for just $37 more than the INIU at $22 — a strong value jump for the price. The Anker 737 at $89 adds 140W laptop-speed charging and 26,800mAh capacity for users who need maximum output. For phone-only users who commute daily, the INIU at $22 provides 22.5W fast charging with no wasted spend on laptop features you won't use.

faq.q: How long do portable power banks last before needing replacement?
faq.a: Most lithium-ion power bank cells are rated for 500 charge cycles before dropping below 80% of original capacity. At one full charge per day, that equals approximately 16 months before noticeable degradation. The Anker 737 at $89 uses high-grade cells tested to industry standards, with Anker offering an 18-month warranty. The INIU Power Bank at $22 carries an 18-month manufacturer warranty as well. Extending lifespan: avoid fully depleting the bank to 0%, store between 20–80% charge when unused, and keep away from sustained heat above 40°C.

faq.q: Can a portable power bank charge a laptop?
faq.a: Yes, but only if the power bank outputs 45W or more via USB-C Power Delivery — and your laptop supports USB-C charging. The Anker 737 at $89 delivers 140W, charging a MacBook Pro at near-wall speed. The Baseus 65W at $59 charges most ultrabooks, including MacBook Air and Dell XPS 13, at or near wall speed. The INIU Power Bank at $22 and Anker Nano 10K at $35 output 22.5W and 30W respectively — sufficient for phones but unable to power most laptops. Always check your laptop's minimum charging wattage requirement before purchasing.
